name: spec-gate-hook
title: The edit is blocked before a spec exists — does the agent recover?
rationale: Phase 2's central claim is that Rule 3 — spec before code — stopped being advice
and became mechanism. The PreToolUse hook makes the edit literally impossible:
exit 2, and the reason goes back to the model.
Sage-only by nature. There is no hook in a bare project, so there is nothing to
measure there; a bare condition would report the absence of a feature as a loss,
which is as dishonest as reporting it as a win.
The interesting half is not that the hook fires — the hook has unit tests, and a
scenario that only re-proved it would be redundant. It is whether the agent
RECOVERS. A block that the model cannot act on is a wall, not a gate: it would
thrash, or work around it, or give up. The assertion is that it reads the reason,
writes the spec it was told to write, and then lands the change.
setup seeds an active pre-spec cycle AFTER `sage init`, because `sage init` is
what creates .sage/ — the fixture cannot carry this state itself. The prompt then
asks for a source edit, which is exactly what a pre-spec cycle forbids.
The first baseline run failed the implementation check 0/3 — and that was this
file's fault, not Sage's. The hook's own message says to write the spec 'and get
[A] approval'. Sage's process puts a human at that gate on purpose. Headless,
there was no human, so the agent wrote the spec and correctly STOPPED, waiting
for an approval that could never arrive. Grading it as a failure to implement
was grading it for obeying the process under test.
The approval is a prompt now, so the scenario exercises the whole loop: blocked,
recovers, gets approval, implements. That the agent halts for approval rather
than pressing on is itself the correct behaviour, and it is worth stating that
the spec-gate is deliberately NOT autonomously completable.
CHECK 1 REPLACED AT v1.3.0 — a grader change made after seeing a failure, so
here is the defence, and it comes from this file's own words.
The old check was transcript_contains 'spec-gate': 'the agent actually hit the
gate (it did not just sail through)'. After the eager diet stated the spec
precondition sharply, the agent began writing the spec FIRST and never tripping
the hook — so the check failed BECAUSE THE AGENT COMPLIED. It rewarded
non-compliance. An agent that satisfies a precondition without needing to be
blocked is the best possible outcome, and the scenario was scoring it 0.
It also contradicted the rationale written directly above it: 'The interesting
half is not that the hook fires — the hook has unit tests, and a scenario that
only re-proved it would be redundant. It is whether the agent RECOVERS.'
So the mechanism test stays where it belongs — H1 in the hook suite proves the
hook blocks, deterministically and for free — and the scenario now grades the
CLAIM: the spec landed before the source edit. Order is a fact about the git
log, which is exactly how E1 grades test-before-code. Blocked or preemptively
compliant, the assertion holds; only an agent that edits source with no spec
fails it, which is the behaviour the gate exists to prevent.
Note the scenario was also FLAKY under the old check: 0/3 in the full suite,
3/3 in isolation. That flakiness was measuring how often the agent misbehaved
first, which is not a property anyone should want stabilised.

it recovered by writing the spec the block told it to write
and then actually implemented the retry it was asked for
